A [general election](/wiki/General_elections_in_the_United_Kingdom "General elections in the United Kingdom") was held in the United Kingdom on Thursday 6 May 2010, to elect 650[[n 2]](#cite_note-5) Members of Parliament (or MPs) to the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"). This was the first election to be held after the minimum [age of candidacy](/wiki/Age_of_candidacy "Age of candidacy") was reduced from [21 to 18](/wiki/Electoral_Administration_Act_2006 "Electoral Administration Act 2006").

It resulted in the [Labour government](/wiki/Brown_ministry "Brown ministry") losing its [66-seat majority](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election"); however, with the opposition [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") ending up with only 306 elected MPs, it was also the first election since that of [February 1974](/wiki/February_1974_United_Kingdom_general_election "February 1974 United Kingdom general election") to result in a [hung parliament](/wiki/Hung_parliament "Hung parliament"). The Conservatives entered government (at first in partnership with the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)")), and remained in office for 14 years until [2024](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election").

For the leaders of all three major political parties, this was their first general election contest as party leader, something that had last been the case at the [1979 election](/wiki/1979_United_Kingdom_general_election "1979 United Kingdom general election"). Incumbent Prime Minister [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") had taken office in June 2007 following the end of [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair")'s 10-year tenure as prime minister and 13 years as leader of the Labour Party, while David Cameron had succeeded [Michael Howard](/wiki/Michael_Howard "Michael Howard") in December 2005 and [Nick Clegg](/wiki/Nick_Clegg "Nick Clegg") had succeeded [Menzies Campbell](/wiki/Menzies_Campbell "Menzies Campbell") (who never contested a general election) in December 2007.

During the campaign, the three main party leaders engaged in the first [televised debates](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election_debates "2010 United Kingdom general election debates"). The Liberal Democrats achieved a breakthrough in opinion polls following the first debate, in which their leader Nick Clegg was widely seen as the strongest performer. Nonetheless, on polling day their share of the vote increased by only 1%, with a net loss of five seats. This was still the Liberal Democrats' largest popular vote since the party's creation in 1988; they found themselves in a pivotal role in the formation of the new government. The share of votes for parties other than Labour or the Conservatives was 35%, the largest since the [1918 general election](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election "1918 United Kingdom general election"). In terms of votes it was the most "three-cornered" election since [1923](/wiki/1923_United_Kingdom_general_election "1923 United Kingdom general election"), as well as in terms of seats since [1929](/wiki/1929_United_Kingdom_general_election "1929 United Kingdom general election"). The [Green Party of England and Wales](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales") won its first ever seat in the House of Commons, and the [Alliance Party of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Alliance_Party_of_Northern_Ireland "Alliance Party of Northern Ireland") also gained its first elected member.[[2]](#cite_note-6) The general election saw a 5.1% [national swing](/wiki/United_Kingdom_general_election_records "United Kingdom general election records") from Labour to the Conservatives, the third-largest since 1945. The result in one constituency, [Oldham East and Saddleworth](/wiki/Oldham_East_and_Saddleworth "Oldham East and Saddleworth"), was subsequently declared void on petition because of illegal practices during the campaign (see [2011 Oldham East and Saddleworth by-election](/wiki/2011_Oldham_East_and_Saddleworth_by-election "2011 Oldham East and Saddleworth by-election")); it was the first such instance since 1910.

A hung parliament had been largely anticipated by the opinion polls in the run-up to the election, so politicians and voters were better prepared for the constitutional process that would follow such a result than they had been in 1974.[[3]](#cite_note-timesonline1-7) The [coalition government](/wiki/Cameron%E2%80%93Clegg_coalition "Cameron–Clegg coalition") that was subsequently formed was the first to result directly from a UK election. The hung parliament came about in spite of the Conservatives managing both a higher vote total and a higher share of the vote than the previous Labour government had done in [2005](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election"), when it had secured a comfortable majority (although vastly reduced from its landslide victories at the previous two elections). [Coalition talks](/wiki/Conservative%E2%80%93Liberal_Democrat_coalition_agreement "Conservative–Liberal Democrat coalition agreement") began immediately between the Conservatives and the Liberal Democrats, and [lasted for five days](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_government_formation "2010 United Kingdom government formation"). There was an aborted attempt to put together a Labour/Liberal Democrat coalition (although 11 seats from other smaller parties would have been required). To facilitate this, Gordon Brown announced on the evening of Monday 10 May that he would resign as Leader of the Labour Party. Realising that a deal between the Conservatives and the Liberal Democrats was imminent, Brown resigned the next day, on Tuesday 11 May, as prime minister, marking the end of 13 years of Labour government.[[4]](#cite_note-news.bbc.co.uk-8) This was accepted by Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"), who then invited David Cameron to form a government in her name and become prime minister. Just after midnight on 12 May, the Liberal Democrats approved the agreement "overwhelmingly",[[5]](#cite_note-New_government_–_live_blog-9)[[6]](#cite_note-LibDemApproveBBC-10) sealing a coalition government of Conservatives and Liberal Democrats.

A total of 149 sitting MPs stood down at the election, the highest since [1945](/wiki/1945_United_Kingdom_general_election "1945 United Kingdom general election"), including many former New Labour Cabinet ministers such as former [Deputy Prime Minister](/wiki/Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Deputy Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") [John Prescott](/wiki/John_Prescott "John Prescott"), [Alan Milburn](/wiki/Alan_Milburn "Alan Milburn"), [Geoff Hoon](/wiki/Geoff_Hoon "Geoff Hoon"), [Ruth Kelly](/wiki/Ruth_Kelly "Ruth Kelly"), [James Purnell](/wiki/James_Purnell "James Purnell") and [John Reid](/wiki/John_Reid,_Baron_Reid_of_Cardowan "John Reid, Baron Reid of Cardowan"). One reason for the very high number of MPs standing down was the [parliamentary expenses scandal a year earlier](/wiki/United_Kingdom_parliamentary_expenses_scandal "United Kingdom parliamentary expenses scandal"). A record 228 new MPs were elected at the election. Many of the Conservative MPs elected for the first time became ministers in government. Notable newcomers who were elected to parliament in 2010 include future Conservative Prime Minister [Liz Truss](/wiki/Liz_Truss "Liz Truss"), future chancellors of the Exchequer [Rachel Reeves](/wiki/Rachel_Reeves "Rachel Reeves"), [Sajid Javid](/wiki/Sajid_Javid "Sajid Javid"), [Kwasi Kwarteng](/wiki/Kwasi_Kwarteng "Kwasi Kwarteng") and [Nadhim Zahawi](/wiki/Nadhim_Zahawi "Nadhim Zahawi"), future Home Secretary [Priti Patel](/wiki/Priti_Patel "Priti Patel"), future Defence Secretary [Gavin Williamson](/wiki/Gavin_Williamson "Gavin Williamson"), future Leaders of the House [Jacob Rees-Mogg](/wiki/Jacob_Rees-Mogg "Jacob Rees-Mogg") and [Penny Mordaunt](/wiki/Penny_Mordaunt "Penny Mordaunt"), future Health Secretary [Matt Hancock](/wiki/Matt_Hancock "Matt Hancock") and future Deputy Prime Ministers [Dominic Raab](/wiki/Dominic_Raab "Dominic Raab") and [Thérèse Coffey](/wiki/Th%C3%A9r%C3%A8se_Coffey "Thérèse Coffey").

On 6 April 2010, the prime minister, [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown"), visited [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace") for a meeting with Queen Elizabeth II to ask her to [dissolve Parliament](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Dissolution of the Parliament of the United Kingdom") on 12 April, confirming in a live press conference in [Downing Street](/wiki/Downing_Street "Downing Street"), as had long been speculated, that the election would be held on 6 May,[[7]](#cite_note-11) five years since the previous election on 5 May 2005. The election took place on 6 May in [649 constituencies](/wiki/Constituencies_of_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constituencies of the Parliament of the United Kingdom") across the United Kingdom, under the [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post "First-past-the-post") system, for seats in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"). Voting in the [Thirsk and Malton constituency](/wiki/Thirsk_and_Malton_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Thirsk and Malton (UK Parliament constituency)")[[note 2]](#cite_note-thirsk-12) was postponed for three weeks because of the [death of a candidate](/wiki/List_of_deaths_of_candidates_during_general_elections_of_the_United_Kingdom "List of deaths of candidates during general elections of the United Kingdom").

The governing Labour Party campaigned to secure a fourth consecutive term in office, and to restore support lost since 2001 due to the [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War").[[8]](#cite_note-13) The Conservative Party sought to gain a dominant position in British politics after losses in the 1990s, and to replace Labour as the governing party. The Liberal Democrats hoped to make gains from both sides and hoped to hold the [balance of power](/wiki/Balance_of_power_(parliament) "Balance of power (parliament)") in a hung parliament. Since the [televised debates](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election_debates "2010 United Kingdom general election debates") between the three leaders, their poll ratings had risen to the point where many considered the possibility of a Liberal Democrat role in Government.[[9]](#cite_note-14) Polls just before election day saw a slight swing from the Liberal Democrats back to Labour and Conservatives, with the majority of final polls falling within one point of Conservatives 36%, Labour 29%, Liberal Democrats 23%.[[10]](#cite_note-15)[[11]](#cite_note-16) However, record numbers of undecided voters raised uncertainty about the outcome.[[12]](#cite_note-17)[[13]](#cite_note-18) The [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party"), encouraged by their victory in the [2007 Scottish parliament elections](/wiki/2007_Scottish_Parliament_election "2007 Scottish Parliament election"), set itself a target of 20 MPs and was hoping to find itself holding a balance of power.[[14]](#cite_note-19) Equally, [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru") sought gains in Wales. Smaller parties which had had successes at local elections and the [2009 European elections](/wiki/2009_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2009 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom") ([UK Independence Party](/wiki/UK_Independence_Party "UK Independence Party"), [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales"), [British National Party](/wiki/British_National_Party "British National Party")) looked to extend their representation to seats in the House of Commons. The [Democratic Unionist Party](/wiki/Democratic_Unionist_Party "Democratic Unionist Party") looked to maintain, if not extend, its number of seats, having been the fourth-largest party in the House of Commons.

### MPs declining re-election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: MPs declining re-election")]

Main article: [List of MPs who stood down at the 2010 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/List_of_MPs_who_stood_down_at_the_2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "List of MPs who stood down at the 2010 United Kingdom general election")

This election had an unusually high number of MPs choosing not to seek re-election, with more standing down than did so at the [1945 general election](/wiki/1945_United_Kingdom_general_election "1945 United Kingdom general election") (which on account of the extraordinary [wartime circumstances](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") came ten years after the preceding election).[[17]](#cite_note-22) This has been attributed to the [2009 expenses scandal](/wiki/2009_expenses_scandal "2009 expenses scandal") and the fact there was talk that redundancy-style payments for departing MPs might be scrapped after the election.[[18]](#cite_note-23)

In all, 149 MPs (100 Labour, 35 Conservatives, 7 Liberal Democrats, 2 Independents, 1 Independent Conservative and 1 member each from the SNP, Plaid Cymru, the DUP, and the SDLP) decided not to contest the election. Additionally, three seats were vacant at the time of the dissolution of Parliament; two due to the deaths of Labour MPs and one due to the [resignation](/wiki/Resignation_from_the_House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "Resignation from the House of Commons of the United Kingdom") in January 2010 of a DUP member.

The hypothetical results of the 2005 election, if they had taken place with the new boundaries

Main articles: [Fifth periodic review of Westminster constituencies](/wiki/Fifth_periodic_review_of_Westminster_constituencies "Fifth periodic review of Westminster constituencies") and [Constituencies of the Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Constituencies_of_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constituencies of the Parliament of the United Kingdom")

Each of the four national [boundary commissions](/wiki/Boundary_commissions_(United_Kingdom) "Boundary commissions (United Kingdom)") is required by the Parliamentary Constituencies Act 1986 (as amended by the Boundary Commissions Act 1992) to conduct a general review of all the constituencies in its part of the United Kingdom every eight to twelve years to ensure the size and composition of constituencies are as fair as possible. Based on the Rallings and Thrasher [studies](/wiki/Notional_election_results "Notional election results") using ward-by-ward data from local elections and the 2005 general election, the new boundaries used in 2010 would have returned nine fewer Labour MPs had they been in place at the previous election; given that there were to be four more seats in the next parliament, this notionally reduced Labour's majority from 66 to 48.[[19]](#cite_note-Media_Guide-24)

Pursuant to Boundary Commission for England recommendations, the number of seats in England increased by four, and numerous changes were made to the existing constituency boundaries.[[20]](#cite_note-25)

Northern Ireland continued to elect 18 MPs, but minor changes were made to the eastern constituencies in accordance with the Northern Ireland Boundary Commission's recommendations.[[21]](#cite_note-26) For the first time, these changes include the splitting of an [electoral ward](/wiki/Electoral_ward "Electoral ward") between two constituencies.

Following the recommendations of the Boundary Commission for Wales, the total number of seats remained at 40, although new seats caused by radical redrawing of boundaries in [Clwyd](/wiki/Clwyd "Clwyd") and [Gwynedd](/wiki/Gwynedd "Gwynedd") were fought for the first time: [Arfon](/wiki/Arfon_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Arfon (UK Parliament constituency)") and [Dwyfor Meirionnydd](/wiki/Dwyfor_Meirionnydd_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Dwyfor Meirionnydd (UK Parliament constituency)") replaced [Caernarfon](/wiki/Caernarfon_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Caernarfon (UK Parliament constituency)") and [Meirionnydd Nant Conwy](/wiki/Meirionnydd_Nant_Conwy_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Meirionnydd Nant Conwy (UK Parliament constituency)"), respectively; [Aberconwy](/wiki/Aberconwy_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Aberconwy (UK Parliament constituency)") replaced [Conwy](/wiki/Conwy_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Conwy (UK Parliament constituency)"). At the time of the election Welsh constituencies had electorates on average around 14,000 smaller than their counterparts in England.[[22]](#cite_note-27)

Scotland saw its most recent large-scale review completed in 2004, so its 59 constituencies remained the same as at the 2005 general election.

## Contesting parties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Contesting parties")]

### Main parties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Main parties")]

See also: [2005 Conservative Party leadership election](/wiki/2005_Conservative_Party_leadership_election "2005 Conservative Party leadership election"), [2006 Liberal Democrats leadership election](/wiki/2006_Liberal_Democrats_leadership_election "2006 Liberal Democrats leadership election"), [2007 Labour Party leadership election (UK)](/wiki/2007_Labour_Party_leadership_election_(UK) "2007 Labour Party leadership election (UK)"), and [2007 Liberal Democrats leadership election](/wiki/2007_Liberal_Democrats_leadership_election "2007 Liberal Democrats leadership election")

All three main parties went into the general election having changed leaders since 2005. [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") became [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") leader in December 2005, replacing [Michael Howard](/wiki/Michael_Howard "Michael Howard"). [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") succeeded [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair") as leader of the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") and prime minister in June 2007. [Nick Clegg](/wiki/Nick_Clegg "Nick Clegg") was elected as leader of the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") in December 2007, succeeding [Menzies Campbell](/wiki/Menzies_Campbell "Menzies Campbell") who had replaced [Charles Kennedy](/wiki/Charles_Kennedy "Charles Kennedy") in January 2006. The last time all three main parties went into a general election with new leaders was in the [1979 election](/wiki/1979_United_Kingdom_general_election "1979 United Kingdom general election"), when [James Callaghan](/wiki/James_Callaghan "James Callaghan") as Labour leader, [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher") for the Conservatives, and [David Steel](/wiki/David_Steel "David Steel") with the then-[Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") took to the polls.

The prospect of a [coalition](/wiki/Coalition_government "Coalition government") or [minority government](/wiki/Minority_government "Minority government") was being considered well before polling day. [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") made comments about the possibility of a coalition in January 2010.[[23]](#cite_note-28) In 2009, it was reported that senior civil servants were to meet with the Liberal Democrats to discuss their policies, an indication of how seriously the prospect of a hung parliament was being taken.[[24]](#cite_note-29) [Nick Clegg](/wiki/Nick_Clegg "Nick Clegg")[[25]](#cite_note-30) and [Menzies Campbell](/wiki/Menzies_Campbell "Menzies Campbell")[[26]](#cite_note-31) had continued the position of [Charles Kennedy](/wiki/Charles_Kennedy "Charles Kennedy") of not being prepared to form a coalition with either main party and of voting against any [Queen's Speech](/wiki/Queen%27s_Speech "Queen's Speech") unless there was an unambiguous commitment in it to introduce [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation").

### Other parties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Other parties")]

See also: [List of parties contesting the 2010 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/List_of_parties_contesting_the_2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "List of parties contesting the 2010 United Kingdom general election")

Other parties with representation at Westminster after the previous general election included the [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party"), with six parliamentary seats, [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru") from Wales with three seats, and [Respect – The Unity Coalition](/wiki/Respect_Party "Respect Party") and [Health Concern](/wiki/Health_Concern "Health Concern"), each of which held one parliamentary seat in England. Since that election, the SNP had won the [2007 Scottish Parliament elections](/wiki/2007_Scottish_Parliament_election "2007 Scottish Parliament election") and gained control of the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government"), and also won the largest share of the [2009 European Parliament election](/wiki/2009_European_Parliament_election "2009 European Parliament election") vote in Scotland.[[27]](#cite_note-32) In Wales, the Labour Party remained the largest party in the [Welsh Assembly](/wiki/2007_National_Assembly_for_Wales_election "2007 National Assembly for Wales election"), although Plaid Cymru increased their share of the vote and formed a [coalition government](/wiki/One_Wales "One Wales") with Labour.[[28]](#cite_note-Labour-Plaid-33)

In 2009 the [Ulster Unionist Party](/wiki/Ulster_Unionist_Party "Ulster Unionist Party") and the Conservative Party announced they had formed an [electoral alliance](/wiki/Electoral_alliance "Electoral alliance") whereby the two parties would field joint candidates for future elections under the banner of "[Ulster Conservatives and Unionists – New Force](/wiki/Ulster_Conservatives_and_Unionists_%E2%80%93_New_Force "Ulster Conservatives and Unionists – New Force")".[[29]](#cite_note-LadyH-34) However, this caused the sole UUP MP Lady [Sylvia Hermon](/wiki/Sylvia_Hermon "Sylvia Hermon") to resign from the party on 25 March 2010, leaving them with no representation at Westminster for the first time in their history.[[30]](#cite_note-35)

Many constituencies were contested by other, smaller parties. Parties that won no representatives at Westminster in 2005 but have seats in the [devolved assemblies](/wiki/Devolution#United_Kingdom "Devolution") or [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") included the [Alliance Party of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Alliance_Party_of_Northern_Ireland "Alliance Party of Northern Ireland"), the [Progressive Unionist Party of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Progressive_Unionist_Party_of_Northern_Ireland "Progressive Unionist Party of Northern Ireland"), the [British National Party](/wiki/British_National_Party "British National Party"), the [UK Independence Party](/wiki/UK_Independence_Party "UK Independence Party") (UKIP), and the Green parties in the UK: the [Green Party of England and Wales](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales"), the [Scottish Green Party](/wiki/Scottish_Green_Party "Scottish Green Party"), and the [Green Party in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Green_Party_in_Northern_Ireland "Green Party in Northern Ireland"). In 2009, [Nigel Farage](/wiki/Nigel_Farage "Nigel Farage") announced his intention to resign as UKIP leader to focus on becoming an MP. Farage was replaced in an election by party members by [Lord Pearson of Rannoch](/wiki/Lord_Pearson_of_Rannoch "Lord Pearson of Rannoch"), whose stated intention was for the electoral support of UKIP to force a hung parliament. The Green Party of England and Wales voted to have a position of leader for the first time; the [first leadership election](/wiki/2008_Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales_leadership_election "2008 Green Party of England and Wales leadership election") was won by [Caroline Lucas](/wiki/Caroline_Lucas "Caroline Lucas"), who successfully contested the constituency of [Brighton Pavilion](/wiki/Brighton_Pavilion_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Brighton Pavilion (UK Parliament constituency)").

In addition, a new loose coalition, [Trade Unionist and Socialist Coalition](/wiki/Trade_Unionist_and_Socialist_Coalition "Trade Unionist and Socialist Coalition") (TUSC), contested a general election for the first time. TUSC was a grouping of left wing parties that participated in the [2009 European Parliament elections](/wiki/2009_European_Parliament_election_in_the_United_Kingdom "2009 European Parliament election in the United Kingdom") under the name of [No2EU](/wiki/No2EU "No2EU"); members included the [Socialist Workers Party](/wiki/Socialist_Workers_Party_(UK) "Socialist Workers Party (UK)"), the [Socialist Party](/wiki/Socialist_Party_(England_and_Wales) "Socialist Party (England and Wales)"), the Socialist Alliance, [Socialist Resistance](/wiki/Socialist_Resistance "Socialist Resistance"), and is supported by some members of [UNISON](/wiki/UNISON "UNISON"), the [National Union of Teachers](/wiki/National_Union_of_Teachers "National Union of Teachers"), the [University and College Union](/wiki/University_and_College_Union "University and College Union"), the [National Union of Rail, Maritime and Transport Workers](/wiki/National_Union_of_Rail,_Maritime_and_Transport_Workers "National Union of Rail, Maritime and Transport Workers"), and the [Public and Commercial Services Union](/wiki/Public_and_Commercial_Services_Union "Public and Commercial Services Union"). Several members of these unions ran as candidates under the TUSC banner.[[31]](#cite_note-36) However, some former members of NO2EU, such as the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK,_1989) "Liberal Party (UK, 1989)")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and the [Communist Party of Britain](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Britain "Communist Party of Britain"),[[32]](#cite_note-37) chose not to participate in the TUSC campaign. The coalition did not run candidates against left wing Labour or Respect candidates.[[33]](#cite_note-38)[[34]](#cite_note-39)

## Campaign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Campaign")]

### April

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: April")]

The prospective Labour candidate for [Moray](/wiki/Moray_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Moray (UK Parliament constituency)"), Stuart Maclennan, was sacked after making offensive comments on his [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") page, referring to elderly voters as "coffin dodgers" and voters in the North of Scotland as "[teuchters](/wiki/Teuchter "Teuchter")", and insulting politicians such as Cameron, Clegg, [John Bercow](/wiki/John_Bercow "John Bercow") and [Diane Abbott](/wiki/Diane_Abbott "Diane Abbott").[[35]](#cite_note-40)

The UKIP candidate for [Thirsk and Malton](/wiki/Thirsk_and_Malton "Thirsk and Malton")—John Boakes—died, causing the election in the constituency to be postponed until 27 May.[[36]](#cite_note-41)[[37]](#cite_note-42)[[38]](#cite_note-43)[[39]](#cite_note-44)

Philip Lardner, the Conservative candidate for [North Ayrshire and Arran](/wiki/North_Ayrshire_and_Arran "North Ayrshire and Arran"), was suspended from the party for comments he made about homosexuality on his website, describing it as not "normal behaviour". [Andrew Fulton](/wiki/Andrew_Fulton_(diplomat) "Andrew Fulton (diplomat)"), the chairman of the [Scottish Conservative Party](/wiki/Scottish_Conservative_Party "Scottish Conservative Party"), called the comments "deeply offensive and unacceptable", adding: "These views have no place in the modern Conservative party." However, he still appeared as a Conservative candidate because it was too late to remove his name from the ballot paper.[[40]](#cite_note-45)

A total of 2,378 postal voters in [Bristol West](/wiki/Bristol_West "Bristol West") were wrongly sent ballot papers for [Bristol East](/wiki/Bristol_East "Bristol East") by mistake. [Bristol City Council](/wiki/Bristol_City_Council "Bristol City Council") officials asked people to tear up the wrong papers and said: "Every effort will be made to ensure delivery [of new ballot papers] by 30 April."[[41]](#cite_note-46)

The SNP attempted but failed to ban the broadcast of the final party leaders' debate in Scotland, in a court action. They had argued that "the corporation [the BBC] had breached its rules on impartiality by excluding the SNP". The judge, Lady Smith, ruled that "the SNP's case 'lacks the requisite precision and clarity'" and added she could not "conclude the BBC had breached impartiality rules". Additionally, broadcasting regulator Ofcom ruled that it had not "upheld complaints received from the SNP and Plaid Cymru about The First Election Debate broadcast on ITV1 on Thursday 15 April 2010".[[42]](#cite_note-47)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Conservative_Party_posters.jpg/250px-Conservative_Party_posters.jpg)](/wiki/File:Conservative_Party_posters.jpg)

An election sign in a residential property.

The leader of the UK Independence Party, [Lord Pearson](/wiki/Malcolm_Pearson,_Baron_Pearson_of_Rannoch "Malcolm Pearson, Baron Pearson of Rannoch"), wrote an open letter to [Somerset](/wiki/Somerset "Somerset") newspapers, asking voters to support Conservative candidates, rather than UKIP candidates in the [Somerton and Frome](/wiki/Somerton_and_Frome "Somerton and Frome"), [Taunton Deane](/wiki/Taunton_Deane_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Taunton Deane (UK Parliament constituency)") and [Wells](/wiki/Wells_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Wells (UK Parliament constituency)") constituencies. This action was criticised by UKIP candidates who refused to stand down.[[43]](#cite_note-48)

The Labour candidate for Bristol East and former MP [Kerry McCarthy](/wiki/Kerry_McCarthy "Kerry McCarthy") revealed information about postal votes cast in the constituency on Twitter. [Avon and Somerset Police](/wiki/Avon_and_Somerset_Police "Avon and Somerset Police") said they were "looking into a possible alleged breach of electoral law". [Bristol City Council](/wiki/Bristol_City_Council "Bristol City Council") stated: "This is a criminal matter and [it] will be for the police to decide what action to take."[[44]](#cite_note-49)

The former Prime Minister [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair") returned to the campaign trail for Labour, visiting a polyclinic in Harrow West, following a troubled Labour campaign.[[45]](#cite_note-50)

Postal voters in the marginal [Vale of Glamorgan](/wiki/Vale_of_Glamorgan_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Vale of Glamorgan (UK Parliament constituency)") constituency had to be issued with new ballot papers after mistakenly being told they did not have to sign applications for postal votes.[[46]](#cite_note-51)

A group of entrepreneurs warned on the dangers of a [Labour-Liberal](/wiki/Liberal-Labour_(UK) "Liberal-Labour (UK)") coalition in an open letter to *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* on 29 April.[[47]](#cite_note-52)[[48]](#cite_note-53)[[49]](#cite_note-54)

#### Bigotgate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Bigotgate")]

On 28 April, [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") met Gillian Duffy, a 65-year-old woman and lifelong Labour voter from [Rochdale](/wiki/Rochdale_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Rochdale (UK Parliament constituency)"), a Labour–Liberal Democrat marginal seat. She asked him about vulnerable people supposedly not receiving benefits because immigrants were receiving them, adding: "You can't say anything about the immigrants because you're saying that you're ... but all these eastern Europeans what are coming in, where are they flocking from?" Brown replied: "A million people have come from Europe but a million British people have gone into Europe."[[50]](#cite_note-55)[[51]](#cite_note-56) In a private conversation with his communications director [Justin Forsyth](/wiki/Justin_Forsyth "Justin Forsyth") following the meeting, Brown described Duffy as "just a sort of bigoted woman that said she used be Labour. I mean it's just ridiculous."[[52]](#cite_note-57)

Brown's remarks were [inadvertently recorded](/wiki/Hot_mic "Hot mic") by a [Sky News](/wiki/Sky_News "Sky News") microphone he was still wearing, and widely broadcast. Labour sources later stated that Brown had misheard Duffy and thought she had asked, "where are they fucking from?"[[53]](#cite_note-58)[[54]](#cite_note-59)[[55]](#cite_note-60) Soon after the incident, Brown talked to [Jeremy Vine](/wiki/Jeremy_Vine "Jeremy Vine") live on [BBC Radio 2](/wiki/BBC_Radio_2 "BBC Radio 2") and publicly apologised to Duffy. American comedian [Jon Stewart](/wiki/Jon_Stewart "Jon Stewart") commented that the clip showed the moment when Brown's "political career leaves his body".[[56]](#cite_note-61) Brown subsequently visited Duffy to apologise in person. Upon emerging, he described himself as a "penitent sinner",[[57]](#cite_note-62) while Duffy refused to speak to the press and would not shake hands with him in front of the cameras. She said the incident had left her feeling more sad than angry and that she would not be voting for Labour or any other party.[[58]](#cite_note-63) The incident was subsequently dubbed "Bigotgate", which was later added to the [Collins English Dictionary](/wiki/Collins_English_Dictionary "Collins English Dictionary").[[59]](#cite_note-64) Despite this, Labour went on to gain the [Rochdale](/wiki/Rochdale_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Rochdale (UK Parliament constituency)") seat from the Liberal Democrats, one of the few gains they made in the election.

### May

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: May")]

In [Hornsey and Wood Green](/wiki/Hornsey_and_Wood_Green "Hornsey and Wood Green") constituency 749 postal voters were sent ballot papers which asked voters to pick three candidates instead of one; [Haringey Council](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Haringey "London Borough of Haringey") had to send correct versions by hand.[[60]](#cite_note-65)

The [Metropolitan Police](/wiki/Metropolitan_Police "Metropolitan Police") launched an investigation in the [London Borough of Tower Hamlets](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Tower_Hamlets "London Borough of Tower Hamlets"). *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* reported on 2 May that the investigation had revealed some names on the register were fictitious, with a late surge in applications to be added to the electoral register (before 20 April deadline) leading to 5,000 additions without time for full checks.[[61]](#cite_note-66) In terms of the outcome of the borough's two seats, the narrower majority in any event exceeded 5,000 votes in [Poplar and Limehouse](/wiki/Poplar_and_Limehouse "Poplar and Limehouse"), at 6,030 votes.

The Labour candidate for [North West Norfolk](/wiki/North_West_Norfolk "North West Norfolk"), Manish Sood, described [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") as Britain's worst ever prime minister.[[62]](#cite_note-67) The comments, which he repeated to a variety of news outlets, took attention away from the previous day's speech by Brown to [Citizens UK](/wiki/Citizens_UK "Citizens UK"), widely described as his best in the campaign.[[63]](#cite_note-68)[[64]](#cite_note-69)

A Conservative Party activist in [Peterborough](/wiki/Peterborough "Peterborough") was arrested after alleged postal voting fraud, calling into question 150 postal votes.[[65]](#cite_note-70)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Nick_Griffin%2C_talking_to_voters.jpg/250px-Nick_Griffin%2C_talking_to_voters.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nick_Griffin,_talking_to_voters.jpg)

[Nick Griffin](/wiki/Nick_Griffin "Nick Griffin"), leader of the [British National Party](/wiki/British_National_Party "British National Party"), talking to voters in [Romford Market](/wiki/Romford_Market "Romford Market").

Simon Bennett resigned as the head of the [British National Party](/wiki/British_National_Party "British National Party")'s online operation then redirected its website to his own on which he attacked the party's leadership.[[66]](#cite_note-71)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Polling_station_6_may_2010.jpg/250px-Polling_station_6_may_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Polling_station_6_may_2010.jpg)

Polling station in [Camberwell](/wiki/Camberwell "Camberwell")

On the morning of polling day, 6 May, the former and later leader of UKIP, [Nigel Farage](/wiki/Nigel_Farage "Nigel Farage"), standing in [Buckingham](/wiki/Buckingham_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Buckingham (UK Parliament constituency)") against the Speaker, was injured when a light banner-towing aircraft in which he was a passenger crashed near [Brackley](/wiki/Brackley "Brackley"), Northamptonshire.[[67]](#cite_note-72)

Groups of voters waiting in queues at 10 pm were locked out of polling stations in [Sheffield Hallam](/wiki/Sheffield_Hallam_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Sheffield Hallam (UK Parliament constituency)"), Manchester and Leeds; and police said one London polling station was open until 10.30 pm, which triggered a national review of polling station requirements led by the [Electoral Commission](/wiki/Electoral_Commission_(United_Kingdom) "Electoral Commission (United Kingdom)").[[68]](#cite_note-73)

The counts for the [Foyle](/wiki/Foyle_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Foyle (UK Parliament constituency)") and [East Londonderry](/wiki/East_Londonderry_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "East Londonderry (UK Parliament constituency)") constituencies were suspended because of a security alert around 11 pm after a car was abandoned outside the counting centre, causing a bomb scare.

The SNP insisted that as the leading political party in Scotland in the latest opinion poll, it should be included in any debate broadcast in Scotland.[[80]](#cite_note-85) On 22 December 2009, the UKIP leader, [Lord Pearson](/wiki/Malcolm_Pearson,_Baron_Pearson_of_Rannoch "Malcolm Pearson, Baron Pearson of Rannoch") stated that his party should also be included. Following a decision by the BBC Trust not to uphold a complaint from the SNP and Plaid Cymru over their exclusion from the planned BBC debate, the SNP announced on 25 April that they would proceed with legal action over the debate scheduled for 29 April.[[81]](#cite_note-86) The party said it was not trying to stop the broadcast but it wanted an SNP politician included for balance. The SNP lost the case, in a judgement delivered on 28 April.[[82]](#cite_note-87)

A polling station in [Wetherby](/wiki/Wetherby "Wetherby"), [West Yorkshire](/wiki/West_Yorkshire "West Yorkshire")

Since each MP is elected separately by the [first past the post](/wiki/First_past_the_post "First past the post") voting system, it is impossible to precisely project a clear election outcome from overall UK shares of the vote. Not only can individual constituencies vary markedly from overall voting trends, but individual countries and regions within the UK may have a very different electoral contest that is not properly reflected in overall share of the vote figures.

Immediately following the previous general election, Labour held a double-digit lead in opinion polls. However, over the course of 2005, this lead was eroded somewhat. By December 2005, the Conservative party showed its first small leads in opinion polls following the controversial [90 days' detention](/wiki/Terrorism_Act_2006#Extending_the_period_of_detention_without_charge "Terrorism Act 2006") proposals and the election of [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") to the leadership of the Conservative party.[[83]](#cite_note-88)

In early 2006, opinion polls were increasingly mixed with small leads given alternately to Labour and Conservative. From the May 2006 local elections, in which Labour suffered significant losses, the Conservatives took a small single-digit lead in opinion polls. Labour regained the lead in June 2007 following the resignation of [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair") and the appointment of [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") as prime minister. From November 2007, the Conservatives again took the lead and, from then, extended their lead into double digits, particularly in response to the MPs' expenses scandal, although there was some evidence that the lead narrowed slightly towards the end of 2009. By the end of February 2010, [Ipsos MORI](/wiki/Ipsos_MORI "Ipsos MORI"), [ICM](/wiki/ICM_Research "ICM Research"), [YouGov](/wiki/YouGov "YouGov") and [ComRes](/wiki/ComRes "ComRes") polls had all found a sufficient narrowing of the Conservative lead for media speculation about a hung parliament to return.[[84]](#cite_note-89)

From 15 April 2010, following the first televised debate of the party leaders, polling data changed dramatically, with the Lib Dem vote proportion rising to 28–33%, and the Conservative vote proportion falling. In some polls, the Liberal Democrats took the lead from the Conservatives. Under UNS projections, this made a hung parliament highly probable, if Lib Dem performance had persisted.[[85]](#cite_note-90)

The following graph shows ComRes poll results recorded over the period 11 April – 6 May 2010, including annotations of the three TV debates:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/33/ComRes_Graph_Election_2010.JPG)](/wiki/File:ComRes_Graph_Election_2010.JPG)

After the second debate on 22 April the polls, on average, placed the Conservatives in the lead on 33%, the Liberal Democrats in second on 30% and Labour in third on 28%. If these polls had reflected the election day results on a uniform swing nationwide, Labour would have had the most seats in a hung Parliament.

### Exit poll

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Exit poll")]

At 22:00 on election day, coinciding with the closure of the polls, the results of an [exit poll](/wiki/Exit_poll "Exit poll") completed by GfK NOP and Ipsos MORI on behalf of the [BBC](/wiki/BBC_News "BBC News"), [Sky](/wiki/Sky_News "Sky News") and [ITV](/wiki/ITV_News "ITV News") news services was announced. Data were gathered from individuals at 130 polling stations around the country.

| Parties | | Seats | Change |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") | 307 | Increase 97 |
|  | [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") | 255 | Decrease 94 |
|  | [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") | 59 | Decrease 3 |
|  | Others | 29 | *N/A* |
| **Hung Parliament**   (Conservatives 19 seats short of overall majority) | | | |

The results of the poll initially suggested a hung parliament with the Conservative Party 19 seats from a controlling majority; this was later adjusted to 21 seats. The distribution of seats between the Conservatives, Labour, Liberal Democrats and other parties was initially suggested to be 307, 255, 59 and 29, respectively,[[86]](#cite_note-91) although the seat numbers were later changed to 303, 251, 69 and 27, respectively.[[87]](#cite_note-BBCLive-92)

Initial reaction to the exit poll by various commentators was of surprise at the apparent poor prospects for the Liberal Democrats[[88]](#cite_note-93) because it was at odds with many opinion polls undertaken in the previous weeks. The actual results showed that the exit poll was a good predictor.

A later BBC Exit poll (05:36 BST) predicted the Conservatives on 306 (20 short of an overall majority), Labour on 262 and Liberal Democrats on 55.[[87]](#cite_note-BBCLive-92)

### Election petitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Election petitions")]

Two results were also challenged by defeated candidates through election petitions – [Fermanagh and South Tyrone](/wiki/Fermanagh_and_South_Tyrone_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Fermanagh and South Tyrone (UK Parliament constituency)"), and [Oldham East and Saddleworth](/wiki/Oldham_East_and_Saddleworth "Oldham East and Saddleworth"). These candidates had lost by 4 and 103 votes respectively.

#### Fermanagh and South Tyrone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Fermanagh and South Tyrone")]

The defeated Unionist 'Unity' candidate, Rodney Connor, lodged a petition against the successful Sinn Féin candidate, [Michelle Gildernew](/wiki/Michelle_Gildernew "Michelle Gildernew"), in [Fermanagh and South Tyrone](/wiki/Fermanagh_and_South_Tyrone_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Fermanagh and South Tyrone (UK Parliament constituency)"), alleging irregularities in the counting of the votes had affected the result. Gildernew had won with a plurality of four votes. However, the court found that there were only three ballot papers which could not be accounted for, and even if they were all votes for Connor, Gildernew would have had a plurality of one. The election was therefore upheld.[[107]](#cite_note-Unionist_dismay-120)

#### Oldham East and Saddleworth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Oldham East and Saddleworth")]

See also: [2011 Oldham East and Saddleworth by-election](/wiki/2011_Oldham_East_and_Saddleworth_by-election "2011 Oldham East and Saddleworth by-election")

On 28 May 2010, the defeated Liberal Democrat candidate [Elwyn Watkins](/wiki/Elwyn_Watkins "Elwyn Watkins") lodged a petition against the election of [Phil Woolas](/wiki/Phil_Woolas "Phil Woolas") (Labour) in [Oldham East and Saddleworth](/wiki/Oldham_East_and_Saddleworth "Oldham East and Saddleworth") constituency.[[108]](#cite_note-121) The petition challenged leaflets issued by Woolas's campaign as having contained false statements of fact concerning Watkins' personal character, which is an illegal practice under section 106 of the [Representation of the People Act 1983](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1983 "Representation of the People Act 1983"). The statements attempted to link Watkins with Muslim extremists and death threats to Woolas, accused him of reneging on a promise to live in the constituency, and implied that his campaign was funded by illegal foreign political donations.[[109]](#cite_note-122)[[110]](#cite_note-123)

During the court case a number of emails between Woolas and his campaign team emerged. In one, Woolas's agent, Joe Fitzpatrick, emailed Woolas and campaign adviser Steven Green, to say: "Things are not going as well as I had hoped ... we need to think about our first attack leaflet."[[111]](#cite_note-124) A reply from Fitzpatrick said: "If we don't get the white vote angry he's gone."[[112]](#cite_note-125) The court hearing finished on 17 September 2010, with the judges reserving their judgement until 5 November 2010.[[113]](#cite_note-126) On that day Woolas was found to have breached section 106 of the Representation of the People Act 1983 in relation to three of the four statements complained about, and the judges ruled that his election was void.[[114]](#cite_note-127) [Phil Woolas](/wiki/Phil_Woolas "Phil Woolas") applied for a judicial review into the ruling,[[115]](#cite_note-128) stating that "this election petition raised fundamental issues about the freedom to question and criticise politicians" and that it "will inevitably chill political speech".[[116]](#cite_note-129) He succeeded in overturning the finding in respect of one of the three statements but the main findings of the election court judgment were upheld. A [by-election](/wiki/2011_Oldham_East_and_Saddleworth_by-election "2011 Oldham East and Saddleworth by-election") on 13 January 2011 resulted in the election of [Debbie Abrahams](/wiki/Debbie_Abrahams "Debbie Abrahams") (Labour).

## Analysis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Analysis")]

For results by county/region and analysis, see [Results breakdown of the 2010 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/Results_breakdown_of_the_2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "Results breakdown of the 2010 United Kingdom general election").

For results by constituency, see [Results of the 2010 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/Results_of_the_2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "Results of the 2010 United Kingdom general election").

For details by MP, see [List of MPs elected in the 2010 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/List_of_MPs_elected_in_the_2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "List of MPs elected in the 2010 United Kingdom general election").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/2010_UK_General_Election_Gallagher_Index.png/250px-2010_UK_General_Election_Gallagher_Index.png)](/wiki/File:2010_UK_General_Election_Gallagher_Index.png)

The disproportionality of the house of parliament in the 2010 election was 15.57 according to the [Gallagher Index](/wiki/Gallagher_Index "Gallagher Index"), with the Liberal Democrats losing out to both the Conservatives and Labour.

At 9:41 on 7 May, the BBC confirmed a hung parliament. The Conservatives stood at 290 seats, Labour at 247 and Liberal Democrats at 51.[[3]](#cite_note-timesonline1-7)[[117]](#cite_note-BBC-Election-2010-web-130) One constituency seat ([Thirsk and Malton](/wiki/Thirsk_and_Malton "Thirsk and Malton")) was contested on 27 May because of the death of the UKIP candidate and was won by the Conservative Party, whilst another seat ([Oldham East and Saddleworth](/wiki/Oldham_East_and_Saddleworth "Oldham East and Saddleworth")) later had its result declared void; Labour won the resulting [by-election](/wiki/Oldham_East_and_Saddleworth_United_Kingdom_general_election_2010_rerun "Oldham East and Saddleworth United Kingdom general election 2010 rerun").

[![pie chart of the election results showing popular vote against seats won, coloured in party colours](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Chartinguk20100506electionvotesandseats.png/250px-Chartinguk20100506electionvotesandseats.png)](/wiki/File:Chartinguk20100506electionvotesandseats.png)

Proportion of seats (outer ring) shown with proportion of votes (inner ring).

The result showed an overall 5.1% swing from Labour to the Conservatives, the third largest national swing achieved in a general election since 1945 and similar to the 5.3% swing achieved by the Conservative leader Margaret Thatcher in 1979.[[117]](#cite_note-BBC-Election-2010-web-130) The 97 net seat gains made by the Conservatives outdid their previous best gains total in [1950](/wiki/1950_United_Kingdom_general_election "1950 United Kingdom general election"), when they gained 85. Labour's loss of 91 seats was worse than their previous greatest loss of seats, when they lost 77 seats in [1970](/wiki/1970_United_Kingdom_general_election "1970 United Kingdom general election").

Of the 532 seats contested in England (a final seat, Thirsk and Malton, was contested on 27 May), the Conservatives won 298 seats and an absolute majority of 61 seats over all other parties combined, securing an average swing of 5.6% from Labour.[[118]](#cite_note-131) Labour did poorly in many Southern areas, notably in the Eastern Region where they won only two of their 14 seats from 2005: [Luton North](/wiki/Luton_North "Luton North") and [Luton South](/wiki/Luton_South "Luton South"). Labour did, however, gain two seats: [Bethnal Green and Bow](/wiki/Bethnal_Green_and_Bow "Bethnal Green and Bow") and [Chesterfield](/wiki/Chesterfield_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Chesterfield (UK Parliament constituency)"). The Conservatives made 95 of their gains in England, but they also suffered three losses, all to the Liberal Democrats. For the Liberal Democrats, their eight gains were overshadowed by their 12 losses – one to Labour and 11 to the Conservatives.

The [British National Party](/wiki/British_National_Party "British National Party") (BNP) would record their highest ever vote in a general election with 1.9% of the popular vote. They would decline after. This is the highest vote for a British [Far-right](/wiki/Far-right_politics_in_the_United_Kingdom "Far-right politics in the United Kingdom") party.

None of Scotland's 59 seats changed hands and all were held by the same party that had won them at the 2005 election, with Labour regaining the two seats they had lost in by-elections since 2005. There was a swing to Labour from the Conservatives of 0.8% (with Labour increasing its share of the vote by 2.5% and the Conservatives increasing by just 0.9%) The Conservatives finished with just a single MP representing a Scottish constituency.

Of the 40 seats contested in Wales, the Conservatives more than doubled their seats from three to eight, taking one from the Liberal Democrats and four from Labour. [Welsh nationalist](/wiki/Welsh_nationalist "Welsh nationalist") party [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru")'s number of seats was reduced from three to two on the new seat boundaries, but they managed to gain one seat, [Arfon](/wiki/Arfon_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Arfon (UK Parliament constituency)"), from Labour. Labour did, however, regain [Blaenau Gwent](/wiki/Blaenau_Gwent_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Blaenau Gwent (UK Parliament constituency)"), which had once been Labour's safest seat in Wales until it had been taken by an Independent, [Peter Law](/wiki/Peter_Law "Peter Law"), in 2005.[[119]](#cite_note-132) Overall, Labour made a net loss of 4 seats but remained the biggest party, with 26.

There were 18 seats contested in [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"). Both [Irish nationalist](/wiki/Irish_nationalist "Irish nationalist") parties, [Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin") and the [Social Democratic and Labour Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_and_Labour_Party "Social Democratic and Labour Party") (SDLP), held their seats. The [unionist](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland") [Democratic Unionist Party](/wiki/Democratic_Unionist_Party "Democratic Unionist Party") (DUP) and [Ulster Unionist Party](/wiki/Ulster_Unionist_Party "Ulster Unionist Party") (UUP) (the latter in [an electoral pact with the Conservatives](/wiki/Ulster_Conservatives_and_Unionists "Ulster Conservatives and Unionists")), lost one seat each. This left the nationalist parties unchanged with eight seats, the main unionist parties with eight seats (all DUP), the Alliance with one seat and an independent unionist with one seat. It is the first time since the [partition of Ireland](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland") that unionist parties failed to secure a majority of Northern Ireland's Westminster seats in a general election, and also the first time Sinn Féin obtained the largest share of the vote in Northern Ireland at a general election.[[120]](#cite_note-133)

### Notable results

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Notable results")]

* [Secretary of State for Children, Schools and Families](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Children,_Schools_and_Families "Secretary of State for Children, Schools and Families") [Ed Balls](/wiki/Ed_Balls "Ed Balls") held his seat of [Morley and Outwood](/wiki/Morley_and_Outwood "Morley and Outwood") by 1,101 votes, despite much anticipation of a "[Portillo moment](/wiki/Portillo_moment "Portillo moment")".[[121]](#cite_note-134) (He went on to lose the seat at the [following election](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election").)
* [Minister of State for Borders and Immigration](/wiki/Minister_of_State_for_Borders_and_Immigration "Minister of State for Borders and Immigration") [Phil Woolas](/wiki/Phil_Woolas "Phil Woolas") retained [Oldham East and Saddleworth](/wiki/Oldham_East_and_Saddleworth "Oldham East and Saddleworth") by just 103 votes. However, following a legal challenge by his Liberal Democrat opponent, [Elwyn Watkins](/wiki/Elwyn_Watkins "Elwyn Watkins"), which found the local Labour campaign to have used negative and [false information](/wiki/Electoral_fraud#Disinformation "Electoral fraud") against Watkins, a new by-election was ordered and held on 13 January 2011. Woolas was barred from standing in this by-election; Watkins stood again, but Labour held the seat with an increased majority.[[122]](#cite_note-BBC_12003985-135)
* Former [Home Secretary](/wiki/Home_Secretary "Home Secretary") [Charles Clarke](/wiki/Charles_Clarke "Charles Clarke") lost [Norwich South](/wiki/Norwich_South "Norwich South") by 310 votes to the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)").[[123]](#cite_note-136)
* Another former Home Secretary, [Jacqui Smith](/wiki/Jacqui_Smith "Jacqui Smith"), lost [Redditch](/wiki/Redditch_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Redditch (UK Parliament constituency)") on an above average 9.2% swing to the Conservatives. She had held the seat since 1997, and the seat was a key Conservative target. Her role in the [2009 expenses scandal](/wiki/2009_expenses_scandal "2009 expenses scandal") contributed to her defeat.[[124]](#cite_note-137)
* [Minister of State for Health](/wiki/Department_of_Health_and_Social_Care "Department of Health and Social Care") [Mike O'Brien](/wiki/Mike_O%27Brien_(British_politician) "Mike O'Brien (British politician)") unexpectedly lost [North Warwickshire](/wiki/North_Warwickshire_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "North Warwickshire (UK Parliament constituency)"), a seat he had held for 18 years, by just 54 votes to Conservative challenger [Dan Byles](/wiki/Dan_Byles "Dan Byles").[[125]](#cite_note-138)
* [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales") leader [Caroline Lucas](/wiki/Caroline_Lucas "Caroline Lucas") won [Brighton Pavilion](/wiki/Brighton_Pavilion_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Brighton Pavilion (UK Parliament constituency)"), becoming their first Westminster MP[[126]](#cite_note-139)[[127]](#cite_note-140)
* [British National Party](/wiki/British_National_Party "British National Party") leader [Nick Griffin](/wiki/Nick_Griffin "Nick Griffin") finished in third place after a heavy loss in [Barking](/wiki/Barking_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Barking (UK Parliament constituency)") to incumbent Labour MP [Margaret Hodge](/wiki/Margaret_Hodge "Margaret Hodge")[[128]](#cite_note-141)
* In the wake of job losses at the local [steel works](/wiki/Tata_Steel_Europe "Tata Steel Europe"), [Solicitor General](/wiki/Solicitor_General "Solicitor General") [Vera Baird](/wiki/Vera_Baird "Vera Baird") lost her seat of [Redcar](/wiki/Redcar_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Redcar (UK Parliament constituency)") on a swing of 21.8% to [Ian Swales](/wiki/Ian_Swales "Ian Swales") of the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)").
* [Glenda Jackson](/wiki/Glenda_Jackson "Glenda Jackson") held [Hampstead and Kilburn](/wiki/Hampstead_and_Kilburn "Hampstead and Kilburn") by just 42 votes, with only 32.81% of the vote, 0.08% ahead of the Conservatives, with the Liberal Democrats trailing less than 1,000 votes behind both other main parties. (The constituency afterward became a [safe](/wiki/Safe_seat "Safe seat") Labour seat in the two elections held towards the end of the decade.)
* [Peter Robinson](/wiki/Peter_Robinson_(Northern_Ireland_politician) "Peter Robinson (Northern Ireland politician)") lost [Belfast East](/wiki/Belfast_East_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Belfast East (UK Parliament constituency)") after 31 years as MP for the constituency, to the [Alliance Party](/wiki/Alliance_Party_of_Northern_Ireland "Alliance Party of Northern Ireland") candidate [Naomi Long](/wiki/Naomi_Long "Naomi Long"). A [scandal](/wiki/Iris_Robinson_scandal "Iris Robinson scandal") involving his wife and fellow MP [Iris Robinson](/wiki/Iris_Robinson "Iris Robinson")'s extramarital affair and her procuring £50,000 for her lover to start a restaurant had led to Robinson's resignation as [First Minister of Northern Ireland](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Northern_Ireland "First Minister of Northern Ireland") earlier that year, and assisted in the almost-23% swing to the Alliance Party.
* [Gisela Stuart](/wiki/Gisela_Stuart "Gisela Stuart") held onto her [Birmingham Edgbaston](/wiki/Birmingham_Edgbaston "Birmingham Edgbaston") seat despite many predictions that she would lose it. This was the only one of the 50 most marginal seats Labour held that was not lost by the party.

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Demographics")]

### Candidate demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Candidate demographics")]

The election resulted in an increase in the number of MPs from ethnic minorities from 14 to 27, including the first black and Asian female Conservative MPs, [Helen Grant](/wiki/Helen_Grant_(politician) "Helen Grant (politician)") and [Priti Patel](/wiki/Priti_Patel "Priti Patel"),[[129]](#cite_note-142) and the first female Muslim MPs, [Rushanara Ali](/wiki/Rushanara_Ali "Rushanara Ali"), [Shabana Mahmood](/wiki/Shabana_Mahmood "Shabana Mahmood") and [Yasmin Qureshi](/wiki/Yasmin_Qureshi "Yasmin Qureshi").[[130]](#cite_note-143) This means that 4.2% of MPs are from an ethnic minority—in the [2001 Census](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_census "2001 United Kingdom census"), it was reported that [ethnic minorities](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_the_United_Kingdom "Ethnic groups in the United Kingdom") comprised 7.9% of the population. The number of female MPs rose to 141, an increase from 19.5% to 21.7% of all MPs, and the highest ever total; the number of female Conservative MPs rose from 18 (8.6% of all Conservatives) to 48 (15.7%).[[131]](#cite_note-144)

### Northern Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Northern Ireland")]

Main article: [2010 United Kingdom general election in Northern Ireland](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election_in_Northern_Ireland "2010 United Kingdom general election in Northern Ireland")

In Northern Ireland a swing of more than 20% resulted in DUP [First Minister](/wiki/First_Minister_and_deputy_First_Minister_of_Northern_Ireland "First Minister and deputy First Minister of Northern Ireland") [Peter Robinson](/wiki/Peter_Robinson_(Northern_Ireland_politician) "Peter Robinson (Northern Ireland politician)") losing his [Belfast East](/wiki/Belfast_East_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Belfast East (UK Parliament constituency)")[[134]](#cite_note-147) seat to the [Alliance Party](/wiki/Alliance_Party_of_Northern_Ireland "Alliance Party of Northern Ireland")'s [Naomi Long](/wiki/Naomi_Long "Naomi Long"), giving Alliance its first elected MP in Westminster.

Sir [Reg Empey](/wiki/Reg_Empey "Reg Empey"), leader of the UUP/Conservative alliance (UCUNF), standing for the first time in [South Antrim](/wiki/South_Antrim_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "South Antrim (UK Parliament constituency)"), lost to the DUP incumbent [William McCrea](/wiki/William_McCrea,_Baron_McCrea_of_Magherafelt_and_Cookstown "William McCrea, Baron McCrea of Magherafelt and Cookstown"). Thus both leaders of the main Unionist parties failed to win seats while the UUP for the first time had no MPs at Westminster. A few days after the election, Empey announced that he would resign before the party conference, triggering a [leadership election](/wiki/2010_Ulster_Unionist_Party_leadership_election "2010 Ulster Unionist Party leadership election").

[Sylvia Hermon](/wiki/Sylvia_Hermon "Sylvia Hermon"), Lady Hermon retained her seat in [North Down](/wiki/North_Down_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "North Down (UK Parliament constituency)"), significantly increasing her percentage of the vote despite a slightly lower turnout and her defection from the UUP/Conservative alliance to stand as an independent.

New SDLP leader [Margaret Ritchie](/wiki/Margaret_Ritchie_(politician) "Margaret Ritchie (politician)"), succeeding [Eddie McGrady](/wiki/Eddie_McGrady "Eddie McGrady") MP, won against Sinn Féin's Caitriona Ruane in [South Down](/wiki/South_Down_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "South Down (UK Parliament constituency)"). All of the Sinn Féin and SDLP incumbents held their seats, although Sinn Féin's [Michelle Gildernew](/wiki/Michelle_Gildernew "Michelle Gildernew") retained her seat in [Fermanagh & South Tyrone](/wiki/Fermanagh_and_South_Tyrone_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Fermanagh and South Tyrone (UK Parliament constituency)") by only four votes over the Independent Unionist Unity candidate, [Rodney Connor](/wiki/Rodney_Connor "Rodney Connor"), after three recounts.[[135]](#cite_note-148)
