## Effect of the expenses scandal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Effect of the expenses scandal")]

Main article: [United Kingdom parliamentary expenses scandal](/wiki/United_Kingdom_parliamentary_expenses_scandal "United Kingdom parliamentary expenses scandal")

Many of the MPs who were most prominently caught up in the scandal decided, or were ordered [*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] not to stand for re-election in 2010. Among them were [Margaret Moran](/wiki/Margaret_Moran "Margaret Moran"),[[136]](#cite_note-149) [Elliot Morley](/wiki/Elliot_Morley "Elliot Morley"),[[137]](#cite_note-150) [David Chaytor](/wiki/David_Chaytor "David Chaytor"),[[138]](#cite_note-151) [Nicholas](/wiki/Nicholas_Winterton "Nicholas Winterton") and [Ann Winterton](/wiki/Ann_Winterton "Ann Winterton"),[[139]](#cite_note-152) [Derek Conway](/wiki/Derek_Conway "Derek Conway"),[[140]](#cite_note-153) [John Gummer](/wiki/John_Gummer "John Gummer"),[[141]](#cite_note-154) [Douglas Hogg](/wiki/Douglas_Hogg "Douglas Hogg"),[[142]](#cite_note-155) [Anthony Steen](/wiki/Anthony_Steen "Anthony Steen"),[[143]](#cite_note-156) [Peter Viggers](/wiki/Peter_Viggers "Peter Viggers"),[[144]](#cite_note-157) [Julie Kirkbride](/wiki/Julie_Kirkbride "Julie Kirkbride") and her husband [Andrew MacKay](/wiki/Andrew_MacKay_(British_politician) "Andrew MacKay (British politician)").[[145]](#cite_note-158)

Where sitting MPs did stand for re-election after their expenses claims were criticised, there were some notable losses. Former Home Secretary [Jacqui Smith](/wiki/Jacqui_Smith "Jacqui Smith") lost her marginal [Redditch](/wiki/Redditch_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Redditch (UK Parliament constituency)") seat, which showed a large 9.2% swing to the Conservatives. Smith had claimed expenses on a large family home in Redditch by declaring her house-share with her sister in London as her main home, which had been described as "near fraudulent" by the former chairman of the committee on Standards in Public life,[[146]](#cite_note-159) although she had only been ordered to apologise rather than repay the money.[[147]](#cite_note-160) Former Home Office minister [Tony McNulty](/wiki/Tony_McNulty "Tony McNulty") lost [Harrow East](/wiki/Harrow_East "Harrow East") to the Conservatives on an 8% swing, after repaying over £13,000 claimed on a second home, occupied by his parents, which was 8 miles away from his primary residence.[[148]](#cite_note-161) [Ann Keen](/wiki/Ann_Keen "Ann Keen") lost [Brentford and Isleworth](/wiki/Brentford_and_Isleworth "Brentford and Isleworth") on a 6% swing, but her husband [Alan Keen](/wiki/Alan_Keen "Alan Keen") retained [Feltham and Heston](/wiki/Feltham_and_Heston "Feltham and Heston"). The couple were criticised for claiming for a second home in central London while rarely staying in their nearby constituency home.[[149]](#cite_note-162)

[Shahid Malik](/wiki/Shahid_Malik "Shahid Malik") lost his [Dewsbury](/wiki/Dewsbury_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Dewsbury (UK Parliament constituency)") seat on a 5.9% swing to the Conservatives. Malik had been required to repay some of his expense claims and, at the time of the election, was under investigation for other claims.[[150]](#cite_note-163) [David Heathcoat-Amory](/wiki/David_Heathcoat-Amory "David Heathcoat-Amory") was one of only two sitting Conservatives to be defeated when he lost [Wells](/wiki/Wells_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Wells (UK Parliament constituency)") to the Liberal Democrats by 800 votes. Heathcoat-Amory was criticised for claiming manure on expenses.[[151]](#cite_note-164) [Phil Hope](/wiki/Phil_Hope "Phil Hope"), who repaid over £40,000 in expenses,[[152]](#cite_note-165) was defeated in his [Corby](/wiki/Corby_and_East_Northamptonshire "Corby and East Northamptonshire") constituency although the swing was lower than the national average at 3.3%.

[Hazel Blears](/wiki/Hazel_Blears "Hazel Blears"), who had paid more than £13,000 to cover [capital gains tax](/wiki/Capital_gains_tax "Capital gains tax") which she had avoided by "flipping" the designation of her main residence,[[153]](#cite_note-166) suffered a large drop in her vote in [Salford and Eccles](/wiki/Salford_and_Eccles "Salford and Eccles"), but was still comfortably re-elected; a 'Hazel must go' candidate won only 1.8%.[[154]](#cite_note-167) Conversely, [Brian Jenkins](/wiki/Brian_Jenkins_(politician) "Brian Jenkins (politician)") lost his [Tamworth](/wiki/Tamworth_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Tamworth (UK Parliament constituency)") seat on a large 9.5% swing despite being described as a "saint" by *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")* on account of his low expenses.[[155]](#cite_note-168) Ironically, his successor in the seat was Conservative [Chris Pincher](/wiki/Chris_Pincher "Chris Pincher"), whose future [sexual assault scandal](/wiki/Chris_Pincher_scandal "Chris Pincher scandal") would [bring down the premiership](/wiki/July_2022_United_Kingdom_government_crisis "July 2022 United Kingdom government crisis") of Prime Minister [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson") twelve years later.[[156]](#cite_note-Labour_MP_of_14_years_loses-169)[[157]](#cite_note-170)[[158]](#cite_note-171)

Predictions[[159]](#cite_note-172) of a rise in the number of successful [Independents](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician") in the election as a result of the [2009 expenses scandal](/wiki/2009_expenses_scandal "2009 expenses scandal") failed to materialise. Independents supported by the [Jury Team](/wiki/Jury_Team "Jury Team") or the [Independent Network](/wiki/Independent_Network "Independent Network"), support networks who both attempted to select and promote high quality Independents who had signed up for the so-called Nolan Principles of public life, set out in the [Committee on Standards in Public Life](/wiki/Committee_on_Standards_in_Public_Life "Committee on Standards in Public Life"), failed to have any significant impact. Broadcaster [Esther Rantzen](/wiki/Esther_Rantzen "Esther Rantzen") gathered a great deal of publicity for her campaign in [Luton South constituency](/wiki/Luton_South_and_South_Bedfordshire "Luton South and South Bedfordshire") where the former MP Margaret Moran had stood down, but ended up losing her deposit in 4th place with 4.4% of the vote; the winner was Moran's successor as Labour candidate.

There was also a high-profile campaign over expenses directed against [Speaker](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_House_of_Commons_(United_Kingdom) "Speaker of the House of Commons (United Kingdom)") [John Bercow](/wiki/John_Bercow "John Bercow"), who had 'flipped' his designation of second home. An imperfectly observed convention states that the major parties do not oppose the Speaker seeking re-election; Bercow faced two main opponents in [Buckingham](/wiki/Buckingham_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Buckingham (UK Parliament constituency)"). Independent former Member of the European Parliament [John Stevens](/wiki/John_Stevens_(English_politician) "John Stevens (English politician)"), standing on the Buckinghamshire Campaign for Democracy ticket, campaigned with a man dressed in a dolphin costume whom he called 'Flipper'[[160]](#cite_note-173) and polled second with 21.4%. Former leader of the [UK Independence Party](/wiki/UK_Independence_Party "UK Independence Party") [Nigel Farage](/wiki/Nigel_Farage "Nigel Farage") also fought the seat but came third in the vote with 17.4%. Bercow won with 47.3%.

## Voting problems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Voting problems")]

Problems occurred with voting at 27 polling places in 16 constituencies, and affected approximately 1,200 people.[[161]](#cite_note-ec-problem-report-174) This situation was condemned by politicians of various parties. [Jenny Watson](/wiki/Jenny_Watson_(civil_servant) "Jenny Watson (civil servant)"), chair of the [Electoral Commission](/wiki/Electoral_Commission_(United_Kingdom) "Electoral Commission (United Kingdom)"), the independent body that oversees the electoral process, was forced on to television to defend preparations and procedures. The Electoral Commission announced it would be carrying out a "thorough investigation".[[162]](#cite_note-turnedaway-175) Under the law in force at the 2010 election, voters had to have been handed their ballots by the 10 pm deadline; people who were waiting in queues to vote at 10 pm were not allowed to vote.[[163]](#cite_note-176)

In [Chester](/wiki/Chester "Chester") there were reports that 600 registered voters were unable to vote because the electoral roll had not been updated,[[164]](#cite_note-177) while in [Hackney](/wiki/Hackney_South_and_Shoreditch_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Hackney South and Shoreditch (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Islington](/wiki/Islington "Islington"), [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds"), [Lewisham](/wiki/Lewisham "Lewisham"), [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle-upon-Tyne_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Newcastle-upon-Tyne (UK Parliament constituency)") and [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield "Sheffield") long queues led to many voters being turned away and unable to vote as the 10 pm deadline arrived.[[162]](#cite_note-turnedaway-175) Some dissatisfied voters staged [sit-ins](/wiki/Sit-in "Sit-in") to protest against what some of them had called "[disenfranchisement](/wiki/Disenfranchisement "Disenfranchisement")".[[162]](#cite_note-turnedaway-175) In [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), higher-than-expected turnout meant several polling stations ran out of ballot papers, with defeated council leader [Warren Bradley](/wiki/Warren_Bradley_(politician) "Warren Bradley (politician)") stating that some residents were unable to cast their votes.[[165]](#cite_note-Liverpool_polling_station_runs_out_of_ballots-178) In Wyre and Preston North, a 14-year-old boy cast a vote after being sent a polling card.[[166]](#cite_note-179)

In parts of Liberal Democrat leader [Nick Clegg](/wiki/Nick_Clegg "Nick Clegg")'s [Sheffield Hallam](/wiki/Sheffield_Hallam_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Sheffield Hallam (UK Parliament constituency)") seat it was reported that students from the city's two universities were placed in separate queues from 'local' residents, who were given priority, resulting in many students being unable to cast their votes.[[167]](#cite_note-180)

Because of [closure of United Kingdom airspace](/wiki/Air_travel_disruption_after_the_2010_Eyjafjallaj%C3%B6kull_eruption "Air travel disruption after the 2010 Eyjafjallajökull eruption") as a result of the [Iceland volcanic eruption](/wiki/2010_eruptions_of_Eyjafjallaj%C3%B6kull "2010 eruptions of Eyjafjallajökull"), potential [expat](/wiki/Expat "Expat") voters in New Zealand were denied a vote when postal voting papers arrived too late to be returned to the UK,[[168]](#cite_note-181) although Australian broadcaster [SBS](/wiki/Special_Broadcasting_Service "Special Broadcasting Service") suggested that given the extremely tight timetabling of overseas votes, *there is very little chance that voting papers* [for voters outside Europe] *will be received, let alone returned, in time to be counted.*[[169]](#cite_note-182)

## Post-election government formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Post-election government formation")]

Main article: [2010 United Kingdom government formation](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_government_formation "2010 United Kingdom government formation")

When it became clear that no party would achieve an overall majority, the three main party leaders made public statements offering to discuss the options for forming the next government with the other parties.

On 11 May 2010, as coalition talks between the Conservatives and the Liberal Democrats seemed to be drawing to a successful conclusion,[[4]](#cite_note-news.bbc.co.uk-8) [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") announced that he was resigning as prime minister and also as Labour leader. He then left [Downing Street](/wiki/Downing_Street "Downing Street"), accompanied by his wife and children, driving to [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace") where he tendered his resignation to the Queen and advised her to call for [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron").[[170]](#cite_note-183)[[171]](#cite_note-184) [Cameron became Prime Minister](/wiki/Premiership_of_David_Cameron "Premiership of David Cameron") one hour after the Queen accepted Brown's resignation.[[172]](#cite_note-CamPMBBC-185) In his first address outside [10 Downing Street](/wiki/10_Downing_Street "10 Downing Street"), he announced his intention to [form a coalition government](/wiki/Cameron%E2%80%93Clegg_coalition "Cameron–Clegg coalition"), the first since the Second World War, with the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)"). As one of his first moves, Cameron appointed [Nick Clegg](/wiki/Nick_Clegg "Nick Clegg") as [Deputy Prime Minister](/wiki/Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Deputy Prime Minister of the United Kingdom").[[172]](#cite_note-CamPMBBC-185)

Just after midnight on 12 May 2010, the Liberal Democrats emerged from a meeting of their Parliamentary party and Federal Executive to announce that the coalition deal had been "approved overwhelmingly",[[5]](#cite_note-New_government_–_live_blog-9)[[6]](#cite_note-LibDemApproveBBC-10) meaning that [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") would lead a coalition government of Conservatives and Liberal Democrats.

Later that day, the two parties jointly published the [Conservative–Liberal Democrat coalition agreement](/wiki/Conservative%E2%80%93Liberal_Democrat_coalition_agreement "Conservative–Liberal Democrat coalition agreement") specifying the terms of the coalition deal.[[173]](#cite_note-186)[[174]](#cite_note-187)[[175]](#cite_note-188)

## Party political and administration costs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Party political and administration costs")]

UK parties spent £31.1m on the campaign of which Conservatives spent 53%, the Labour Party spent 25% and the Liberal Democrats 15%.[[176]](#cite_note-189) Figures from returning officers show that the average administration cost per constituency was £173,846 meaning the average cost per vote was £3.81.[[177]](#cite_note-tony-190)
